coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic performance of CdZnS nanoparticles and attapulgite/carbon nitride composites by carbon quantum dots mediated facilitated charge separation/transfer: Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 production
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Nan
Cao, Jin
Liu, Yani
Xu, Lei
Shi, Xiaojun
Xie, Xuan
Peng, Hui
Zhang, Zhe
coredata.dc:description: In this work, CdZnS@carbon quantum dots/amino-modified attapulgite/carbon nitride (CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN), function as Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts, have been successfully prepared by the hydrothermal method. CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 was optimized toward reduction of Cr(VI) and production of H2O2 under visible light, showing significantly improved photocatalytic performance. After coupling of M-ATP/PCN-1 with CdZnS and a moderate addition of CQDs, the synergistic formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions photocatalytic system effectively enhanced the photocatalytic properties of the composites. The results revealed that the CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN catalysts contain rich active centers and suitable energy band structures, which enable them to produce more OH and O2 −. Additionally, they also absorb visible light strongly and thus effectively improves the photocatalytic activity. Specifically, the reduction of Cr(VI) by CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 catalyst was achieved greater than 97.6 %, along with the production of 719.32 μmol·L−1 H2O2. Combining density functional theory (DFT) calculations, experimental test results and characterizations, we find that the excellent photocatalytic performance is attributed to the incorporation of CQDs and the formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions. We have developed a non-precious metal-based efficient Z-scheme photocatalytic system using simple hydrothermal synthesis method, which has a very high potential for energy production, environmentally sustainable remediation, and water purification.

(a) FT-IR spectra of PCN, M-ATP/PCN-1, CQDs, and CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN-1. (b) Powder XRD patterns of CdZnS, CdZnS/M-ATP/PCN-1, and CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN-1 catalysts.
XPS survey spectra (a) of M-ATP/PCN-1, CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1. High-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) S 2p, (e) Zn 2p, and (f) Cd 3d.

(a) Adsorption and photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by different samples effect of different, (b) initial pH values, (c) scavenging measurements to detect the active species during photo-reduction of Cr over CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, (d) H2O2 production concentration over CdZnS and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, (e) The photocatalytic H2O2 production under different air conditions, and (f) Recycle experiments for the photoreduction of Cr(VI).

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic performance of CdZnS nanoparticles and attapulgite/carbon nitride composites by carbon quantum dots mediated facilitated charge separation/transfer: Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, CdZnS@carbon quantum dots/amino-modified attapulgite/carbon nitride (CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN), function as Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts, have been successfully prepared by the hydrothermal method. CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 was optimized toward reduction of Cr(VI) and production of H2O2 under visible light, showing significantly improved photocatalytic performance. After coupling of M-ATP/PCN-1 with CdZnS and a moderate addition of CQDs, the synergistic formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions photocatalytic system effectively enhanced the photocatalytic properties of the composites. The results revealed that the CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN catalysts contain rich active centers and suitable energy band structures, which enable them to produce more OH and O2 −. Additionally, they also absorb visible light strongly and thus effectively improves the photocatalytic activity. Specifically, the reduction of Cr(VI) by CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 catalyst was achieved greater than 97.6 %, along with the production of 719.32 μmol·L−1 H2O2. Combining density functional theory (DFT) calculations, experimental test results and characterizations, we find that the excellent photocatalytic performance is attributed to the incorporation of CQDs and the formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions. We have developed a non-precious metal-based efficient Z-scheme photocatalytic system using simple hydrothermal synthesis method, which has a very high potential for energy production, environmentally sustainable remediation, and water purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by organic matter, heavy metals, and pathogens has been a major threat to human existence [1,2]. Among these water contaminants, Cr(VI) is a poisonous and mutagenic substance, hazardous to the environment and humans, and difficult to remove from water. On the other hand, Cr(III) is far less toxic than Cr(VI), and its removal from water by physical and chemical methods is relatively easier [3,4]. Therefore, the conversion of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) is often considered an effective method for wastewater treatment and environmental remediation [5–7]. Modern water treatment techniques include adsorption [8,9], biodegradation [10,11], and photocatalytic technology [7,12]. Among these, the photocatalytic degradation method has recently been very popular as a cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and sustainable technology. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a multifunctional chemical that is generally used in chemical, pharmaceutical, printing, and other research fields [13–15]. At present, the principal methods for large-scale production of H2O2 include anthraquinone autoxidation [16], isopropanol oxidation [17], and electrochemical synthesis [18]. However, these traditional methods are disadvantageous as they aggravate environmental pollution and consume high energy, and hence, are not conducive to the sustainable production of H2O2. In this regard, an economical, environment-friendly, and promising alternative can be the application of semiconductor materials to produce H2O2 by photocatalysis [19,20]. Generally, the photocatalytic reaction process involves the separation and transfer of carriers (electron-hole pairs) driven by excited light and redox reactions on the surface of catalysts [19]. Using semiconductor photocatalysts, H2O2 can be produced from water and oxygen by redox reactions. Photo-excited e− can induce O2 to generate H2O2 via an indirect sequential two-step singleelectron (1e−ORR) and/or direct single-step two-electron (2e−ORR), while h+ can also participate in the water oxidation reaction pathway in the single-phase system [21,22].

Polymer carbon nitride (PCN) is the most representative class of photocatalysts, which is widely used due to its unique π-conjugated polymer semiconductor structure, narrow bandgap of 2.7 eV, non-toxicity, and low cost [15,23]. Nevertheless, it possesses some obvious disadvantages, such as a high complexation rate of photogenerated carriers, low specific surface area, and low quantum yield. Several strategies have been developed to ameliorate these drawbacks, such as coupling with narrow bandgap semiconductors [24,25], doping with metal/nonmetal elements [26,27], and clay mineral coupling [28]. Attapulgite (ATP) is a silicate natural mineral clay with a unique rod-like morphology and high specific surface area, rich in surface active sites, which has been widely used in adsorption, decolourisation, and medicine [29,30]. Embedding ATP in PCN not only prevents the coalescence of PCN and increases its specific surface area but also enhances the number of its active sites [31].

CdZnS is a ternary sulfide solid solution, possessing the advantages of both the narrow band gap CdS (2.4 eV) and wide band gap ZnS (3.6 eV). A great deal of research has been done, owing to its flexible band gap and band edge positions, strong visible light response, and high resistance to photocorrosion [32,33]. On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging carbon nanomaterials, usually less than 10 nm in diameter, which are widely used in photocatalysis due to their excellent electrical conductivity and electronic cycling capabilities [34]. In addition, CQDs is also advantageous in terms of easy synthesis, non-toxicity, and low cost, which have attracted the attention of researchers [35,36]. Recently, CQDs has been combined with different semiconductors to enhancing visible light absorption and charge transfer efficiency of semiconductor photocatalysts, such as CQDs-CdS [34], g-C3N4/CQDs/CdIn2S4 [37], and CdS/CQDs/BiOCl [38]. Moreover, CQDs can be used as electron mediators in Z-scheme heterojunctions.

In this study, M-ATP/PCN material, compounded with modified attapulgite (M-ATP) and PCN as a carrier, was utilized to couple CQDs and CdZnS on its surface via a hydrothermal method. It led to the formation of CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst, which was further applied for efficient photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and H2O2 generation. The photocatalytic performance of CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN was significantly improved, compared with M-ATP/PCN and CdZnS separately. Further analysis combined with DFT calculations revealed that the CQDs not only act as excellent electron transport mediators sandwiched between CdZnS and M-ATP/PCN but also improve the overall light absorption of the samples. This work provides a strategy for the effective design of futuristic Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalytic systems for environmental protection and wastewater treatment.

Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (1 mmol), CdCl2·2.5H2O (1 mmol), and TAA (5 mmol) were dissolved completely in 50 mL water via sonication. Then, 4.5 mmol NaOH was added, and the samples were then added to a high-pressure reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 18 h. The collected product was washed and dried at 60 °C.

To prepare CQDs, 2.01 g citric acid was dissolved in 20 mL water, 670 μL of ethylenediamine was added, and the mixture was transferred to a high-pressure reaction kettle, to carry out the reaction at 150 °C for 5 h. The resulting brown-black transparent solution was dialyzed in water for 48 h, and the obtained brown powder was freeze-dried.

The preparation of M-ATP/PCN composites was described in our previous work [39,40]. Firstly, 5.0 g melamine was added to 300 mL of distilled water, stirred thoroughly and then x g (x = 0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 and 4.0 g) of M-ATP was added to the above mixed solution and stirred and sonicated for 30 min each at room temperature. Then, the sample was dried at 105 °C for 12 h and ground into powder, and then calcinated at 550 °C (heating rate 5 °C/min) for 4 h. The production was labeled as PCN and M-ATP-x.

To investigate the effect of different carriers on the catalyst, appropriate amounts of CdZnS, 10 % (of the total mass) CQDs, and 20 mg of any one of the PCNs (PCN, M-ATP/PCN-1, M-ATP/PCN-2, M-ATP/PCN-3, and M-ATP/PCN-4) were added to 100 mL of water. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min and then transferred to an autoclave. The reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 12 h, and the products were washed and dried at 60 °C. The synthesized materials were labeled as CdZnS@CQDs-1/PCN, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-2, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-3, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-4, respectively.

Appropriate amounts of CdZnS, y mg (y = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50) M-ATP/PCN-1, and 10 % (of total mass) of CQDs were dissolved in 100 mL of water, sonicated, and transferred to an autoclave to react for 12 h at 180 °C. The washed products were collected and dried. The synthesized materials were labeled as CdZnS@CQDs-1, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 (y%) (y = 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50).

After determining the optimal amount of M-ATP/PCN-1 addition, to investigate the effect of the addition of different amounts of CQDs on the catalyst, an appropriate amount of CdZnS, 20 mg of M-ATP/PCN-1, and z% (of the total mass; z = 0, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50) CQDs were dissolved into 100 mL of water, sonicated and transferred to an autoclave to carry out the reaction at 180 °C for 12 h. The washed product was collected and dried. The as-synthesized materials were labeled as CdZnS/M-ATP/PCN-1, CdZnS@CQDs-z/M-ATP/PCN-1 (z = 1 (10 %), 2 (20 %), 3 (30 %), 4 (40 %) and 5 (50 %)).

In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 10 mg of catalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of mixed solvent (45 mL of ultrapure water and 5 mL of isopropanol as a sacrificial agent). The solution was stirred for 30 min under dark conditions. A 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used as the visible light source. Aliquots (2 mL) were collected every 5 min during light irradiation, filtered through a 0.45 μm Millipore microporous filter, and the amount of H2O2 was determined using the iodometric method. In brief, 1 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 C8H5KO4 and 0.4 mol L−1 KI solution was added to the collected 2 mL aliquot, left for 30 min for complete reaction, and then the H2O2 content was measured at 350 nm band using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2800) [20].

For probing Cr(VI) reduction in the presence of different photocatalysts, 10 mg of catalyst was added to 50 mL of 20 mg L−1 Cr(VI) solution and, stirred for 30 min under dark conditions to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then light irradiation was started, and aliquots were collected every 10 min for determination of the Cr(VI) concentration by diphenylcarbazide spectrophotometry. Briefly, 1 mL of the supernatant was taken out from the aliquots and mixed with 9 mL of deionized water, 0.1 mL of 9.2 M H2SO4 solution, and 0.1 mL of 7.3 M H3PO4 solution. The mixture was injected into a glass vial, and 0.4 mL of diphenylcarbazide solution was added to obtain a purple solution in a few minutes. The absorbance was measured at 540 nm to determine Cr(VI) concentration in the aliquot [3].

The SEM images of PCN and M-ATP/PCN-1 are shown in Fig. S1, which exhibited a porous structure on the surface of the M-ATP/PCN-1 but absent for PCN (smooth surface). This indicates that the doping of M-ATP effectively increases the specific surface area of PCN and gives rise to a higher number of active sites for the subsequent complexation of CdZnS. Fig. 1 depicts the SEM and TEM images of the CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 sample. It is evident from Fig. 1a that the pure CdZnS was uniformly-grown and distributed as nanoparticles having a particle size of tens of nanometers. The surface morphology of CdZnS@CQDs-1 (Fig. 1b) was similar to CdZnS due to the low content of CQDs. In the case of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 (Fig. 1c), the pores of M-ATP/PCN-1 were filled with CdZnS nanoparticles, which indicates the successful coupling of M-ATP/PCN-1 with CdZnS. Fig. 1d reveals the tight coupling of CQDs and M-ATP/PCN-1 with CdZnS and the formation of a tight multiphase interface between these materials. This may eventually retard the complexation of photogenerated carriers and improve photocatalytic performance. As observed from Fig. 1e, the size of CdZnS for CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 was about 15 nm, and its lattice spacing corresponding to the (101) crystal plane was 0.31 nm [41]. The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 (Fig. 1f) confirmed the presence of C, N, Zn, Cd, and S. Each element was uniformly dispersed throughout the scanned area, which further confirms the successfully loading of CQDs and PCN on CdZnS.

Fig. 2a shows the FT-IR spectra of PCN, and the feature appearing near 1600–1200 cm−1 was attributed to the characteristic stretching vibration of the conjugated CN ring. Another characteristic band located at 806 cm−1 arose due to the stretching vibration of the s-triazine ring. Both these features also appeared in the FT-IR spectrum of M-ATP/PCN-1 and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite [28], indicating the successful coupling of M-ATP/PCN-1 with CdZnS. On the other hand, the CQDs possessed a band centered at 3437 cm−1 and three characteristic peaks at 1656 cm−1, 1564 cm−1, and 1412 cm−1, respectively, corresponding to the stretching vibration of OH and the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching of the carboxylic acid group [37]. The characteristic peaks at 3437 cm−1, 1614 cm−1, and 1412 cm−1 were observed for CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite, which indicates the presence of CQDs in the CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite, consistent with its TEM images.

The crystal structure of the sample was investigated via XRD characterization (Fig. 2b). The characteristic peaks at 25.3°, 27.4°, 28.7°, 44.8°, 47.5°, and 53.2° corresponding to the (100), (002), (101), (110), (103), and (112) crystallographic planes, respectively, were observed for pure CdZnS [42]. In the case of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite, in addition to the above-mentioned characteristic peaks for CdZnS, a weak peak at 13.4° corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of M-ATP/PCN-1 was also found [39]. However, the characteristic peak of the (100) crystal plane of M-ATP/PCN-1 was not observed separately, as it overlaps with the 27.4° characteristic peak of CdZnS [37]. Most importantly, for the CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite with different masses of CQDs (Fig. S2a), no significant difference was observed in the XRD patterns, which indicates that the introduction of CQDs does not affect the crystal structure of CdZnS. However, with the increase of M-ATP/PCN-1 incorporation, the changes in the characteristic XRD peaks were evident from Fig. S2b, indicating that the excessive introduction of M-ATP/PCN-1 affects the crystal structure and formation of CdZnS.

The chemical compositions of the surface and the internal bonding interactions of CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, and M-ATP/PCN-1 composites were investigated by XPS. Fig. 3a reveals that the CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite was composed of carbon, nitrogen, cadmium, zinc, and sulfur. Its high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, Cd 3d, Zn 2p and S 2p spectra are also shown in Fig. 3. The C 1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 3b) with binding energies of about 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.4 eV, which were attributed to the CC bond, sp2 hybridized C (NCN), and CN or CO bond, respectively [43,44]. The peak areas of 284.7 eV and 288.3 eV in the case of CdZnS@CQDs-1 composite increased, while the peak area of 286.3 eV decreased, as compared to CdZnS. However, for CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, the peak areas of 284.7 eV and 288.4 eV decreased, and 286.1 eV increased, which may be due to the formation of chemical bonds between M-ATP/PCN-1, CQDs and CdZnS. The N 1s spectrum of M-ATP/PCN-1 was deconvoluted into four peaks with maxima at 398.6 eV, 399.5 eV, 401.2 eV, and 404.6 eV corresponding to sp2 hybridized nitrogen (C-N=C), tertiary nitrogen [N-(C)3], amino (−NH2) nitrogen and π-excitation, respectively. On the other hand, the N 1s spectrum of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite exhibited only two deconvoluted peaks at 404.9 eV and 399.5 eV. High resolution S 2p, Zn 2p, and Cd 3d. XPS spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. 3d, e, f. The major peaks of CdZnS were located at binding energies of 161.7 eV (S 2 p3/2), 162.8 eV (S 2p1/2), 1044.9 eV (Zn 2p1/2), 1022.1 eV (Zn 2p3/2), 405.2 eV (Cd 3d5/2), and 411.9 eV (Cd 3d3/2), corresponding to the valence states −2, +2, and +3, respectively [33,38,45]. For CdZnS@CQDs-1 and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, all the peaks of S 2p, Zn 2p, and Cd 3d slightly shifted toward lower binding energies, indicating that some chemical bonding interactions exist between CdZnS, CQDs, and M-ATP/PCN-1.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. 4a. The doping of M-ATP induced a red-shift of the absorption band in the case of M-ATP/PCN-1 as compared to PCN. The absorption band of CdZnS was located at 593 nm, which was significantly blue-shifted in the cases of CdZnS@CQDs-1 (549 nm) and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 (557 nm) which may originate from the interfacial interaction between CdZnS, CQDs and M-ATP/PCN-1. Among these materials, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 exhibited significantly higher visible light absorption, which is favorable for generating more photogenerated electrons and holes. Therefore, CQDs effectively improved the visible light absorption for the composite. Photoluminescence spectra were also recorded to investigate the behavior of photogenerated carriers in the prepared samples. The CdZnS sample possessed a strong emission peak at 532 nm (Fig. 4b), which suggests that the separation efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs of CdZnS is low. In contrast, the CdZnS@CQDs-1 sample exhibited a much lower fluorescence intensity, indicating that CQDs induce an effective separation of photogenerated carriers. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 enhanced compared to CdZnS@CQDs-1. This indicates that the addition of M-ATP/PCN-1 results in the transfer of electrons from M-ATP/PCN-1 to CdZnS via CQDs, which recombines with the holes of CdZnS, i.e., a Z-scheme electron transfer takes place [6,46]. The optical band gaps of different samples were calculated from the graph (Tauc graph) of (ahν)2 versus (hν) (Fig. 4c). The band gaps of CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, M-ATP/PCN-1, and PCN composites were 2.48 eV, 2.46 eV, 2.34 eV, 2.78 eV, and 3.02 eV, respectively. Moreover, time-resolved fluorescence attenuation spectra are also shown in Fig. 4d, and transient fluorescence lifetimes of 2.14 ns, 1.51 ns, and 1.42 ns were concluded for CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, respectively. This further confirms a higher photogenerated carrier separation rate for CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 [47].

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm experiments were performed to understand the specific surface area and pore structure of PCN, M-ATP/PCN-1, CdZnS, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1. As shown in Fig. S4a, all of these samples exhibited typical type-IV adsorption-desorption hysteresis lines, and in combination with the pore size distribution plots (Fig. S4b), it can be seen that all the samples have a typical mesoporous structure. The Specific surface areas of PCN, M-ATP/PCN-1, CdZnS, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 were 10.28, 30.24, 49.82, and 51.39 m2 g−1 concluded for, respectively. The large specific surface area and abundant mesoporous structure of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 provide good carriers for hosting more active sites and therefore provide favorable conditions for the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency [48].

Transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were performed for further insights into the efficiency of separation of the photogenerated carriers in the individual samples. Notably, higher photocurrent densities and lower charge transfer resistances indicate more efficient separation of electron-hole pairs and hence better photoresponse. [49,50]. It is evident from Fig. 4e that CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 exhibited higher photocurrent density than CdZnS and CdZnS@CQDs-1. This suggests a higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and enhanced photoresponse for CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, which may be related to its unique heterojunction structure. Results of EIS measurements (Fig. 4f) revealed that the arc radii of CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 decreased sequentially, indicating that CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 have lower charge transfer resistance, which leads to increased charge separation efficiency and improved photocatalytic performance.

The photodegradation performance of materials can be evaluated by studying the reduction of Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation. The reduction of Cr(VI) in the presence of M-ATP/PCN (Fig. 5a) was only 1.6 % after 40 min of visible light irradiation (after reaching adsorption equilibrium under dark conditions), while the reduction of Cr(VI) by CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 was greater than 97.6 % under similar conditions. This was not only much higher than that of M-ATP/PCN but also 1.18 times higher than that of CdZnS. The optimal ratio of CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN catalysts was then investigated. First, we investigated the effect of different ratios of M-ATP/PCN (20 %) on the photocatalytic performance of the composites. As shown in Fig. S5a, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 (20 %) showed the best photocatalytic performance, with a reduction rate of 84.0 % at 60 min. Then, different amount of M-ATP/PCN-1 was coupled with CdZnS@CQDs, and their photocatalytic activities were compared (Fig. S5b) to find the most suitable ratio. The photocatalytic performances of these samples increased first and then decreased, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 (20 %) exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance. The same trend of Cr(VI) reduction efficiency was also found after adding different amounts of CQDs (Fig. S5c), and the highest efficiency was achieved for CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 when 10 % (of the total mass) CQDs is added. Fig. S5d–f shows the effect of the ratio of each component of CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN on the photocatalytic performance of the composites for H2O2 production. The best H2O2 production was achieved when M-ATP/PCN-1 was combined with CdZnS and CQDs. The photocatalytic efficiency showed a tendency of increasing and then decreasing with the increase of M-ATP/PCN-1 content, and the best performance of the composites was achieved at the addition of 20 mg of M-ATP/PCN-1. With the increase of CQDs content, the efficiency of H2O2 production also showed a tendency of increasing and then decreasing. Among them, and higher content of CQDs may lead to the agglomeration of the material, which is unfavourable to the rapid progress of the photoreaction. The experimental results showed that CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 produced H2O2 with the highest efficiency of 719.32 μmol·L−1. Next, the optimum acidity of the system to trigger the best photocatalytic performance of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 was investigated. Fig. 5b shows that the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of the catalyst increased with the acidity of the solution, and its highest photoreduction efficiency was achieved at an acidity of 3. This may be due to the fact that pH affects the adsorption of Cr(VI) on the catalyst surface, which in turn affects the catalytic efficiency [51,52]. Additionally, the stability of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite photocatalyst was also assessed by conducting several cycles of photoreduction experiments. Fig. 5f indicates that the CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 photocatalyst could still maintain a reduction efficiency of more than 80 % after carrying out four consecutive cycles. The XRD analysis of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 after 4 cycles (Fig. S6) showed that the structure of the catalyst did not undergo any significant changes after the reaction. This assures its application worthy prospects toward Cr(VI) reduction.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 samples for the production of H2O2 was also evaluated in an air atmosphere. Fig. 5d shows that CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 produced 719.32 μmol·L−1 of H2O2 after 60 min of visible light irradiation, which was 1.79 and 1.27 times more than that was produced in the presence of CdZnS and CdZnS@CQDs-1, respectively. The results showed that the complexation with M-ATP/PCN-1 and the addition of appropriate amount of CQDs could effectively improve the photocatalytic activity of the catalysts, which may be due to the formation of heterojunctions as well as the Z-scheme photocatalytic system. Interestingly, the photocatalytic production of H2O2 increased further when the reaction solution was fed with O2 (Fig. 5e). Nevertheless, CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 photocatalyst produced a considerable amount of H2O2 in pure water as well (Fig. 5e).

The active species involved in the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) was determined using radical capture experiments. TBA, p-BQ, EDTA-2Na and AgNO3 were selected as the trapping agents for hydroxyl radicals (OH), superoxide radicals (O2 −), photoexcited holes (h+), and photoexcited electrons (e−), respectively [53]. It is evident from Fig. 5c, that photocatalytic efficiency of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 photocatalyst was significantly reduced when AgNO3 (that traps photoexcited electrons) is added to the Cr(VI) solution. However, the addition of TBA, p-BQ, and EDTA-2Na had very little effect on the performance of the photocatalyst. This indicates that the photoexcited electron (e−) is the main active species in the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by this photocatalyst. During the photocatalytic reaction, the large amount of e− produced reacts with O2 to produce O2 −. Meanwhile, photogenerated h+ accumulated on CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 and reacted with H2O to form OH [54]. Electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were carried out on CdZnS and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 samples to understand the photocatalytic mechanism further (Fig. 6 ). The intensity of DMPO-O2 − and DMPO-OH signals was much higher in the methanol system under visible light irradiation for CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1, as compared to that of the CdZnS. It suggests that CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 composite can produce more OH and O2 −, which may be due to its higher efficiency to separate photoinduced electron-hole pairs in Z-scheme heterojunctions [55,56].

Moreover, to understand the mechanism of photocatalysis, it is important to know the valence and conduction band positions of the catalysts. Therefore, the valence band positions were first calculated for the samples by the following equation: E NHE = Φ + VBmax − 4.44, where E NHE , Φ and VBmax represent the potential versus the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) (eV), the electron work function obtained from XPS analysis (4.63 eV), and valence band maximum, respectively [14,57]. The valence band (VB) maxima of CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 samples were calculated (from the data in Fig. S3) to be 1.58 eV, 1.43 eV, and 1.41 eV, respectively. From the analysis of the absorbance spectrum, the band gaps of CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 samples were obtained as 2.48 eV, 2.46 eV, and 2.34 eV, respectively (Fig. 4c). Using these values, the calculated conduction band positions of CdZnS, CdZnS@CQDs-1, and CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 catalysts were −0.90 eV, −1.03 eV, and −0.93 eV, respectively.

The mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and generation of H2O2 was investigated in detail using DFT calculations. Combining the results of material characterization and theoretical calculations, the structure of the composite CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN was confirmed (Fig. 7a), and the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction made the structure very stable. As shown in Fig. S7, compared to PCN, CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN shows two sharp characteristic peaks below the Fermi energy level, indicating the presence of strong polarized covalent bonds in the material. The differential charge density map of CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN is shown in Fig. 7b, and a significant charge redistribution was found on the CQDs, especially at the two different materials at the interface, which favors the adsorption of O2 during the reaction process, attributed to the excellent electrical conductivity and the ability to store/release electrons of CQDs, in addition to the presence of uptake on CdZnS and M-ATP/PCN.

The reaction kinetics and thermodynamics of PCN and CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN photocatalysts were investigated by DFT calculations. Fig. 7a and b show the structure diagram and differential charge density map of CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN, respectively. The calculation of the differential charge diagram (Fig. 7b) shows that CQDs form a negative charge accumulation region between CdZnS and PCN layers, and the large conjugated structure of CQD itself enables charge localization between layers, providing a channel for charge transfer between layers (usually regarded as the medium of charge conduction in direct Z-scheme heterojunction), and speeding up the charge transfer between layers. At the same time, it can be seen that there is a significant difference in the amount of charge accumulated in some positive charge regions where CdZnS and PCN are in contact with CQDs, which enables the formation of an internal electric field at the interface to accelerate the transfer of photogenerated charge. The Gibbs free energies of the reaction process of pure PCN and CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN composites at U = 0 V and U = 1.23 V are given in Fig. 7c and d, respectively. The reaction structure of PCN with two upslope steps during the reaction process at U = 0 V (Fig. 7c), which includes the *OOH to *O and *O to *OH steps, and the corresponding ΔG⁎O and ΔG⁎OH were calculated to be 0.86 eV and 11.29 eV, respectively. CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN has three uphill steps during the reaction process, including the steps of *O2 to *OOH, *OOH to *O, and *O to *OH, and the corresponding ΔG⁎OOH, ΔG⁎O and ΔG⁎OH were 1.75 eV, 2.03 eV and 3.52 eV, respectively. Although CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN had three uphill steps, the energy barrier of the *O to *OH step was greatly reduced compared to the reaction of PCN. When U = 1.23 V (Fig. 7d), the energy barriers of all three uphill steps in the reaction process of CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN were further reduced with ΔG⁎OOH, ΔG⁎O, and ΔG⁎OH of 0.52 eV, 0.80 eV, and 2.29 eV, respectively. The structure of the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) of CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN is illustrated in Fig. 7e. The above analysis indicates that under visible light irradiation, the prepared CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN catalyst is more favorable for the reaction than PCN. Therefore, the CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN catalysts have strong selectivity and H2O2 generation performance.

A possible Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism was proposed for on the CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 heterostructure based on the above results, which is shown in Fig. 8 . It suggests that electron-hole pairs are generated in CdZnS and M-ATP/PCN-1 after irradiation of visible light, and electrons get transferred from the conduction band of M-ATP/PCN-1 to the CQDs and recombine with the holes on the valence band of CdZnS, which is also consistent with the photoluminescence results [58]. Here, the CQDs not only acted as excellent electron transfer mediators sandwiched between CdZnS and M-ATP/PCN-1 but also improved the light absorption of the sample effectively. Again, the conduction band of CdZnS@CQDs-1/M-ATP/PCN-1 was more negative than the O2/H2O2 potential (−0.68 V vs NHE) and the Cr(VI)/Cr(III) potential (−0.51 V vs NHE). Therefore, its e − on the conduction band can be donated to Cr(VI) and easily reduce it to Cr(III) or the e− can be utilized to generate H2O2 [15,59,60]. Therefore, a plausible mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) and generation of H2O2 by CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN photocatalysts can be represented as follows (Eqs. (1)–(7)):

In summary, a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst based on CdZnS@CQDs/M-ATP/PCN heterostructure has been successfully prepared. It exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity toward Cr(VI) reduction (97.6 %, 40 min) and H2O2 production (719.32 μmol·L−1, 1 h) under visible light irradiation and retained its stability for four consecutive cycles, which is significantly better than pure Cd pure CdZnS and M-ATP/PCN. This superior photocatalytic activity was attributed to the presence of rich active centers, suitable energy band structure, and enhanced production of OH and O2 −. Moreover, the CQDs acted as electron transport mediators bridging between CdZnS and M-ATP/PCN, which was sufficient to effectively improve the light absorption properties of the samples This work has demonstrated a useful strategy for the development of a non-precious metal-based Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalytic system, which will be beneficial for environmental remediation and water purification.
